**Investment View**

• Valuation: **25x FY28E P/E**

**Business Positioning**
• Premium precision gears & safety-critical components
• Export-led model: **~70% exports (Q3FY26)**
• FY25 geography: Europe **52%**, India/APAC **46%**
• Segment mix: 2W **39%**, CV **17%**, PV **13%**, ATV/RTV **13%**

**Q3FY26 Performance**
• Revenue **₹137.3 cr (+22% YoY, +14% QoQ)**
• EBITDA **₹31.3 cr (+31% YoY)** | Margin **22.8%**
• PAT **₹16.3 cr (+149% YoY, +35% QoQ)**
• Strong earnings acceleration & margin recovery

**Growth Drivers & Orderbook**
• FY27 revenue guidance: **~₹565 cr (+15–20%)**
• BMW EV ramp-up, premium 2W OEM scaling, KTM recovery
• US truck EPS components via ZF
• Multi-year premium orderbook; sales visibility **~₹1,000 cr by FY29–30E**

**Financial Trajectory (FY25–28E)**
• Revenue CAGR **~17.8%**
• EBITDA CAGR **~22.7%**

• FY27 capex **₹77 cr** (efficiency + selective expansion)

**Valuation Comfort**
• FY28E P/E **20.7x** | EV/EBITDA **12x**
• Improving margins + deleveraging support re-rating potential

**Investor Takeaway**
• Strong export-led niche franchise
• Multi-year order visibility
• Earnings compounding story with improving balance sheet

**BHARTI AIRTEL – JM Financial Deep Dive (1 Mar 2026)**

**Rating & Valuation**
• Maintain **BUY**
• Target Price at **₹2,455** (~**30.7% upside**)
• FY28 EV/EBITDA at TP: **10.1x (consol.)**
• SoTP fair value at **₹3,120** (14% IRR)

**Core Thesis**
• **14–15% EBITDA CAGR** over next 2–3 years
• **10–11% ARPU CAGR** expected
• Implied FY28 FCF yield ~**5.5%**

**Key Concerns Addressed**

• **NBFC Foray**
• Equity commitment capped at **₹200 bn**
• First-year spend only **₹15–20 bn**
• Consolidated FCF at **₹600–700 bn p.a.**
• Seen as natural adjacency leveraging **370 mn+** customer base

• **Promoter Stake Overhang**
• Holding reduced to ~**48.9%**
• ~**8.5%** more likely to be offloaded gradually
• Promoters aim to move back toward **50%** over time

• **Tariff Hike Timing**
• Base case: hike within next **6 months**
• Delay to CY27 = only **3–4% EBITDA downside**
• ARPU needs to reach **₹295–325 by FY28** for healthy RoCE

**Financial Trajectory (FY24–28)**
• Revenue: **₹1,514 bn → ₹2,717 bn**
• EBITDA: **₹797 bn → ₹1,491 bn** (margin ~55%)
• PAT: **₹138 bn → ₹598 bn**
• ROE: **17.2% → 33.4%**

**Sensitivity**
• ₹10 ARPU change = **₹45/share** valuation impact
• 10 mn subs change = **₹35/share** impact

**Investor View**
• Structural ARPU re-rating story
• Limited downside in worst-case tariff delay
• Strong FCF + compounding earnings profile

reports.text:
Iran Oil Shock Challenges JPMorgan’s Bearish Dollar View

• 🛢️ Oil Surge Impact: Rising Brent prices amid U.S.–Iran tensions are supporting the US dollar.

• 💵 Strategy Shift Risk: JPMorgan’s earlier bearish dollar / bullish high-beta FX positioning faces pressure.

• 🛡️ Safe-Haven Demand: Geopolitical risk is driving flows into the dollar and haven assets.

• 📉 Risk Currencies Hit: Euro, yen and oil-importer currencies under stress as energy costs rise.

reports.text:
China Well-Prepared for Potential Iran Oil Disruption

• 🇨🇳 High Dependence: Around 90% of Iran’s crude exports go to China.

• 🛢️ Strong Inventory Buffer: Independent refineries hold ample near-term supply.

• 🚢 Oil in Transit Support: Iranian crude already on the way could cover ~5 months of demand.

• 🏦 Massive Strategic Reserves: China has built ~200 days of import cover, aided by discounted Iran/Russia oil.

• ⚠️ Disruption Impact Limited: Even if flows are hit, China’s supply risk appears manageable.

🟡 Impact: Neutral to Slight Positive
Strong buffers reduce immediate oil supply risk for China and help limit global shock fears.

🚀 **KESAR PETROPRODUCTS – Turnaround to Structural Growth Story**

🔹 **Shift from Crude to High-Margin Pigments**
Moved from low-margin CPC crude (1–2%) to downstream pigments (15–18% margins).
9M FY26:
• EBITDA up ~74% YoY
• PAT up ~85% YoY
• EBITDA margin crossed 16%

**Clear operating leverage visible.**

🔹 **Waste-to-Wealth Fertilizer Integration**
Converting pigment by-products into complex fertilizers.
Commercial production started.
At ~75% utilization, fertilizer alone can add 20–25% additional revenue.

🔹 **Co-Generation Plant = Structural Cost Savings**
50% coal reduction.
₹3–4 Cr+ annual savings expected.
Margin kicker from FY27.

🔹 **Strong Guidance**
• 100% bottom-line growth target for FY26
• 15–16% EBITDA margin
• 20% topline growth next year
• 18–20% CAGR over 3 years

🔹 **Promoter Confidence**
₹38 Cr capital infusion via warrants to support expansion.

**📌 Big Picture:**
The company is transitioning from survival to structured growth better product mix, stronger margins, integrated ecosystem.

Around 90% of Iran’s crude exports go to China, but the country is well prepared for disruption.

Small independent refineries hold ample near-term supply, while Iranian oil already in transit could cover roughly five months of demand. China has also built large reserves—about 200 days of import cover—helped by discounted crude from Iran and Russia.

Bottom line: even if Iranian flows are disrupted, the impact on China is likely manageable.

reports.text:
Midcap IT Double Down on AI-Led Mega Deals to Take on Tier-1 Players  

Strategic Pivot to AI-Led Large Deals  
- Mid-tier IT firms are aggressively shifting to AI-first, large multi-year deals to stay relevant amid GenAI disruption and changing client expectations.  
- Narrative is moving from experimentation to AI-native core transformation, with clients demanding clear, outcome-linked value.  

Big Wins for Zensar, LTIMindtree, Coforge  
- Zensar Technologies has bagged a $210 million, 5.5-year deal with a large financial services player, centred on AI-native enterprise transformation and automation.  
- LTIMindtree has secured a $100 million European healthcare contract, along with a large AI-driven tax analytics modernisation deal in India, underscoring its data and AI depth.  
- Coforge has won a $158 million, five-year UK deal and forged a strategic partnership with US-based VHC Health to drive AI-led infrastructure, cloud and digital workplace transformation. 

From Pilots to Full-Scale AI Deployment  
- Clients are moving beyond PoCs and pilots to enterprise-wide AI rollouts, embedding AI into core systems and operations.  
- Boardroom focus is on governance, resilience, operational efficiency and measurable ROI, making AI-linked KPIs central to contract structures.  

Europe Emerges as a Hotspot  
- Deal sizes and win frequency are rising, particularly in Europe, where enterprises are favouring phased modernisation with modular AI capabilities.  
- Midcaps are leveraging speed, flexibility and domain focus to win against larger incumbents in competitive RFPs.  

Competitive Dynamics with Tier-1 IT  
- The shift to AI-embedded, longer-tenure contracts is blurring the line between midcaps and large-cap IT on deal profile.  
- Differentiation is increasingly driven by AI platforms, execution speed and ability to co-innovate rather than just scale and legacy relationships.  

Revenue Visibility and Growth Construct  
- Multi-year, AI-centric deals are becoming the core engine for revenue visibility, supporting better growth predictability for midcap IT.  
- As AI becomes non-discretionary spend, these contracts can lift annuity revenues, expand wallet share and support margin resilience over the cycle.  

Key Takeaway  
AI-led mega deals are turning midcap IT into serious contenders to Tier-1 firms, with long-tenure, outcome-linked contracts poised to structurally rerate growth and visibility for the segment.

Semicon 2.0 to Focus on Chip Design and Deep-Tech Innovation  

Strategic Shift  
- Union IT Minister Ashwini Vaishnaw unveils Semicon Mission 2.0 with focus on design ecosystem and deep-tech startups.  
- Moves beyond manufacturing-led Semicon 1.0 to prioritize chip design, innovation, and equipment manufacturing.  

Core Objective  
- Build a robust semiconductor design ecosystem enabling Indian startups to develop advanced chips and IPs.  
- Government aims to foster companies that could become India’s Qualcomm, Broadcom, or Nvidia.  

Talent & Capability Push  
- India faces a 2 million talent gap in the semiconductor industry—seen as a major opportunity for engineers and students.  
- Initiative to invest in design infrastructure, training, and R&D to close this skills gap.  

Progress So Far  
- 10 semiconductor plants approved under the national mission.  
- One plant already commercial, and another nearing production start.  

Next Steps  
- Align chip design output with domestic fabs for Made-in-India semiconductor production.  
- Scale up equipment manufacturing and design infrastructure to complete the semiconductor value chain.  

Key Takeaway:  
Semicon 2.0 marks India’s pivot from fabs to innovation—aiming to transform the country into a global semiconductor design powerhouse.

Nvidia Teams Up with Telcos to Build AI-Native 6G Networks  

Strategic Alliance  
- Nvidia partners with Nokia, SoftBank Group, and T-Mobile US to co-develop AI-native 6G infrastructure.  
- Target: reinvent how future mobile networks handle AI-driven workloads and intelligent devices.  

Why It Matters  
- Current 5G systems optimized for voice and data—not designed for the coming wave of AI traffic.  
- 6G networks will integrate AI at the core, supporting robots, autonomous systems, and real-time analytics.  

Technology Vision  
- Future architecture to feature AI-powered software, open systems, and programmable radios.  
- Shifts from hardware-heavy designs to cloud-like networks that can self-manage, scale, and adapt rapidly.  

Business Angle  
- Positions Nvidia to expand its AI chips and software ecosystem into telecom infrastructure.  
- Represents a new growth frontier, merging data center intelligence with wireless technology.  

Industry Implication  
- Early collaboration aims to define global 6G standards capable of supporting large-scale AI deployment.  
- Signals the start of convergence between AI computing and next-gen connectivity.  

Key Takeaway:  
Nvidia is moving beyond data centers—embedding AI directly into future telecom networks to power the intelligent, connected economy of the 6G era.

@Stocksip

Volvo Car India Drops 2030 All-Electric Goal, Adopts Flexible EV Path  

Strategic Shift  
- Volvo scraps its earlier target to go 100% electric by 2030.  
- New approach: market-driven transition based on infrastructure, policy, and demand.  
- MD Jyoti Malhotra says electrification commitment remains strong but timeline is now flexible.  

Current Performance  
- EVs form 25% of Volvo India sales, well above the segment average of 10–11%.  
- Goal: raise EV share to ~33% of total sales through new launches and ecosystem expansion.  

New Models & Portfolio  
- Two new EVs planned for 2026 — one SUV and one sedan.  
- Company to continue dual strategy: ICE + mild hybrids alongside EVs.  

Sales Trend  
- 2023: 2,120 units | 2024: 1,861 units | 2025: 1,630 units.  
- Early 2026 (till Mar 1): 307 units sold, indicating cautious momentum.  

Ecosystem Investments  
- Focus on ultra-fast charging infra (Mumbai–Nashik corridor pilot).  
- Strengthening after-sales network and parts availability.  

Positioning & Outlook  
- Aims to sustain premium brand status via safety, Scandinavian design, and sustainability.  
- Faces rising EV competition in India’s luxury segment.  

Key Takeaway:  
Volvo is recalibrating its EV roadmap—prioritizing flexibility, infrastructure readiness, and profitability over rigid timelines.

reports.text:
**BERNSTEIN – India Strategy: Iran Conflict & Equity Impact**

**Immediate Market Impact**
• Expect **1–2% Nifty decline** on open
• Risk-off sentiment to dominate near term
• Prolonged escalation may push Nifty below **24,500**
• Remain **Neutral on Nifty**

**Oil Shock – Core Risk Variable**
• $10/bbl rise = **$12–13bn** extra annual import bill
• $30/bbl spike = **>70 bps GDP hit**
• **50%** of India’s oil imports via Hormuz
• Risk to inflation, fiscal math & rate cuts

**External Linkages At Risk**
• $40–50bn non-oil exports via MENA exposed
• **9mn+ Indians** in MENA; remittances at risk
• EPC & infra projects in region may see delays
• Airlines exposed due to route disruptions

**Sector View**
• Higher risk: **OMCs, Travel, Trade-linked, EPC with MENA exposure**
• Neutral stance on these segments
• Broader market cautious

**Conflict Duration – Key Variable**
• If resolved within **2–3 days**, macro hit limited
• Likely conflict duration **<1 week**
• Prolonged escalation increases downside risk

reports.text:
SEPC Limited – Update on Independent Audit Order | Neutral Financial Impact

SEPC Limited (NSE: SEPC | BSE: 532945) has acknowledged the interim order dated February 19, 2026 from the Hon’ble High Court of Madras in connection with ongoing litigation involving GPE (India) Ltd and others.

🔹 The Court has ordered interim attachment of ₹154.63 crore out of reported receivables of ₹449.62 crore as part of enforcement proceedings.
🔹 PriceWaterhouseCoopers (PWC) has been appointed to conduct a focused review of dues payable to banks/financial institutions and assets available with SEPC, with a report due on March 23, 2026.

SEPC is unlikely to face material financial strain:
- SEPC’s exposure is fully covered under a 2015 indemnity agreement with co-respondent M/s Twarit Consultancy Services Pvt Ltd.
- Twarit is contractually responsible for liabilities arising from this litigation.
- ₹164.5 crore has already been paid by Twarit under the same indemnity framework.
- The current claim of ₹154.63 crore is covered under this arrangement.

➡️ Accordingly, the company has stated that the matter results in a neutral financial impact on SEPC.

The lenders’ consortium has also approached the Court to safeguard secured creditor rights. SEPC continues to operate as a going concern, remains compliant with SEBI listing norms, and is fully cooperating with the audit process.

Background:
The dispute stems from a 2010 agreement (to which SEPC was not a party) and a subsequent 2015 agreement involving SEPC. The matter arose in 2017 and has since progressed through a Singapore arbitral tribunal and Indian courts, leading to the current stage.

*Goldman Sachs on ICICI Lombard*
Recommendation: Neutral, Target ₹1,925 
Industry growth inflected in Q3  
Motor growth outpaced industry in Q3  
Company motor combined ratio materially better than industry  
Health: strategic investment phase, near-term drag acceptable

*Jefferies India Strategy - Mahesh Nandurkar*
Middle East Conflict - Impact likely to be short-term
India's economic links with the Middle East are strong, with the region taking 17% of India's exports and providing 55% of its crude oil and 38% of worker remittances
A prolonged conflict, alongside a large jump in energy prices, would be a major macro negative
However, recent regional conflicts have been temporary, and a dip could be a buying opportunity
Immediate negative for oil marketing cos, travel & hospitality, and rate-sensitive names; positive for Defence
L&T (25+% of consol/40%+ EPC orderbook), Newgen (30% of revenues) have large exposures
Other cos with meaningful (5-10%) exposures include consumer cos (Dabur, Titan), select pharma cos (Ajanta Pharma, Biocon, Cipla)
Major hospital chains (8-10% revenue from international patients); select others (PB Fintech, Voltas)

*BofA India watch – Rahul Bajoria*
West Asia conflict puts a shadow on near term sentiment  India has an estimated 9.7 million people working and living in West Asia  
India gets at least 35% of its remittances ($132 billion annual) from West Asia according to RBI  
Number likely to be north of 40% given small economy exposures  
India imports 45% of its energy from West Asia, bulk of it from GCC economies  
India currently is running with roughly three weeks of oil inventories for oil  
A $10/bbl increase in energy prices adds $12-15 billion per year to current account balance, and 25-30 bps to headline inflation  
India exported $67 billion to West Asia in 2025, accounting for 15% of overall goods exports  
This includes gem and jewelry, plant & machinery, refined petroleum products, automobiles, and food products

*Antique on Middle East conflict*
Global technology selloff pulled down Indian IT services stocks  
Palantir earnings highlighted AI replacing traditional software tools  
AI platforms reducing ERP implementation timelines sharply  
Market sentiment weakened due to automation concerns  
Large part of coding and testing work faces productivity pressure  
Around 9–12% of industry revenue seen at risk  
ERP and third-party software work may also shrink sharply  
New AI partnerships expected to drive future opportunities

*Nomura on GDP* 
India GDP revisions: slower past, faster present  
Revisions show a slower post-pandemic recovery in consumption and informal sectors, a smaller size of the nominal GDP, but faster current growth  
Expect India’s economic cycle to improve in the coming quarters  
This will reflect tailwinds from past policy easing, higher wage growth, and calming of trade tensions  
Expect FY27 GDP growth at an above-consensus 7.1% year-on-year

reports.text:
Key question for investors - should one chase these price spikes by adding more oil exposure?
On argument that current Iran conflict could also be short-lived (as with that in June 2025), medium-term investors can look to use the near-term volatility to add OMC into a correction and book profits on upstream names (ONGC)

*Bernstein on Eternal*
O-P, TP Rs 370
Heightened competitive intensity, slowing QC growth and AI-questions on FD have led to a 20% price correction in last few weeks.
Recent correction offers a good buying opportunity with a favorable risk-reward ratio for a 12-18 mth time-period as key risks are priced in
Near term (2-3 qtrs) growth and profit trajectory may retain some volatility basis how competitive scenarios unfold.

*Jefferies on PSU banks*
PSU banks are leading Pvt ones on growth (14% vs. 13%) with higher growth in housing & auto loans.
While this is partly funded by use of surplus LDR, PSUs are also paying higher rates on TDs.
So, they saw higher compression in spreads than Pvt despite lower share of EBLR loans.
A slight pullback on growth & realigned deposit cost could lift ROA.
Given correlation between ROA & PB, expect that to aid rerating
SBI is among top picks, PNB is BUY

*Jefferies on Cement Sector*
Cement px were flattish MoM in Feb (+2% YoY) & +1.5% YoY for 4QTD (est: 1.5-2%).
Vols have held steady in Jan-Feb & Industry expects strong momentum (+vol push) in March.
Following recent surge in Crude, Petcoke is already higher 20%+.
Based on this fuel trend (& further spike possible), cement cos would require 2%+ (or more) price hike at the start of April, which is possible after year-end vol push recedes
Still stay constructive; like UTCEM, ACEM, JKCE.

*JPMorgan on Dixon Tech*
Recommendation Overweight, Target ₹13700
Mobile PLI 2.0 would be a bit different than the existing scheme
Believe the Street has been assuming that the PLI scheme would not be extended
Dixon's mobile business would see a 50bps margin reduction from FY27
If the scheme were to be extended, Dixon could continue to enjoy a 50bps margin benefit
This could lead to 12-16% EPS upgrades over FY27-28

*Goldman Sachs on India autos* 
February retail tracker  2W and PV volumes rose 28% and 26% YoY respectively; 3-month rolling growth at ~20% each  
CV volumes grew 32% YoY, led by LCV (+28%) and MHCVD (+42%)

reports.text:
*Nomura on Bharti Airtel*
Buy, TP Rs 2300
Investor call Key takeaways
1) committed Airtel Money (unlisted) investment of INR200bn will be done gradually over years with 10-15% to be invested in first year;
2) Co will adopt a progressive dividend policy going forward as FCF generation accelerates;
3) Singtel (ST SP, not rated) may continue to sell its 7% treasury shares in Bharti Airtel gradually over next few years as they consolidate their investments in Associate companies;
4) Bharti Airtel will likely continue to increase its stake in Indus Towers from 51% currently, & have Board approval for another 5% stake;
5) British Telecom stake will continue to be with promoter entity & there are no plans for Bharti Airtel to buy promoter stakes in BT
6) Bharti Airtel will consolidate its stake in Airtel Africa (63% currently) by purchasing 16% stake from promoter family

*CITI on Bharti Airtel*
Buy, TP Rs 2380
Co’s Chairman outlined long‑term strategic priorities in a detailed & transparent investor interaction, helping alleviate concerns following announcement of its NBFC foray  last week that triggered a 6% stock correction
His remarks offered clarity on roadmap for core India business, calibrated expansion into financial services, capital allocation discipline amid robust FCF generation, & plans for key subsidiaries
Chairman also addressed promoter shareholding trajectory, signalling an orderly approach to potential stake reductions by both promoter groups
While uncertainty around timing of next tariff hike may limit near‑term catalysts, believe stock’s recent under-performance presents an enhanced buying opportunity for investors with a longer-term investment horizon.

*CLSA on IT*
Salesforce reported organic revenue growth of c.8.5% for FY26 and has guided for 7-8% organic growth in FY27, implying a deceleration, while IT service companies like CTSH, CAP and EPAM have guided for higher growth next year
This suggests that AI is leading to a net positive volume opportunity for system integrators (SIs), with a preference for build over buy & lower costs of production, where SIs could potentially eat into SaaS
Within coverage, see Persistent as having highest exposure to Salesforce & Infosys leading on Salesforce expertise
Reiterate HC O-PF on Persistent & O-PF on Infosys.

*CITI On IT*
NSEIT has corrected ~19% CYTD, underperforming NIFTY by 16% CYTD
A few common points of discussion among investors
(a) Enterprises will need help with AI implementation – services should benefit;
(b) IT services still very relevant – so why the panic?
(c) Valuations & FCF yields are reasonable;
(d) Indian stocks are more expensive than global peers
Lower target multiples given increased risks to medium-term growth & terminal growth assumptions, driving 14-29% target price changes
Maintain cautious view
Still rank Infosys & HCLT over other large caps

*UBS on Billionbrains Garage Ventures (Groww)*
Initiate Neutral, TP Rs 185
1) Believe high growth phase for Groww's broking business is over (a 79% CAGR in FY23-25) with broking revenue to record a 17% CAGR in FY26-28E.
2) Groww is diversifying into non-broking businesses, including Margin Trading Facility (MTF), wealth management (WM) and credit by leveraging its platform
Believe the non-broking segment will be a growth driver with revenue recording a 59% CAGR in FY26-28E
3) Expect costs to normalise, enabling operating leverage and margin expansion
4) However, believe current valuation of 37x 1Y forward P/E largely reflects the positives

*JPM on Oil & Gas*
Higher oil prices are good for upstream companies - ONGC (N) and bad for the OMC (BPCL/IOCL/HPCL).
Diesel cracks can also increase, which will be good for Reliance and standalone Indian refining stocks.
A spike in natural gas prices can hurt margins for city gas companies (MAHGL - N, IGL/GUJGA - UW), and increase the attractiveness of GAIL’s HH purchase contracts

Goldman’s Strait‑of‑Hormuz Scenarios: How a One‑Month Closure Could Hit Oil

Full closure, no offsets  
- A full one‑month closure of flows through the Strait with no offsets (no spare pipelines, no SPR release) would lift the fair value of oil prices by +$15/bbl.  

Full closure, pipelines only  
- With the same full closure but all 4 mb/d of spare pipeline capacity used, the fair‑value impact moderates to +$12/bbl.  

Full closure, pipelines + SPR  
- If, in addition, global SPRs are released at 2 mb/d for one month, the fair‑value uplift falls further to +$10/bbl.  

Partial 50% closure, pipelines used  
- For a partial 50% one‑month closure with full spare‑pipeline use, Goldman estimates a +$4/bbl hit to fair‑value pricing.  

Partial 25% closure, pipelines used  
- Under a partial 25% one‑month closure, again with all spare pipelines running, the fair‑value impact is just +$1/bbl.  

Key takeaway:  
A full one‑month Strait closure could add roughly $10–15/bbl to fair‑value oil prices, but pipeline diversions and coordinated SPR releases can materially cut the spike, making policy‑driven buffers a key downside risk to any headline‑driven rally.

Bharti Airtel In Focus: Sunil Mittal’s Capital Allocation & Growth Vision

Dividend & shareholder returns  
- Management will progressively increase dividends over time, prioritising shareholder payouts once balance sheet comfort is secured.  
- FY25 already saw a more than 2x payout versus FY24, with guidance for further incremental hikes, not one‑off spikes.  

Core business: India and Africa  
- Capex will stay focused on mobility, broadband (fixed + mobile), and enterprise in India and Africa, without ceding share in either rural or urban.  
- Domestic ARPU is guided towards ₹300 in the near‑term, with ₹350 as the new goal post, driven by value‑over‑volume discipline and 5G‑plus data growth.  
- Vodafone Idea will find it structurally difficult to gain share in an 8–9% YoY growth sector, especially while operating under balance‑sheet stress.  

Data‑centre strategy (Nxtra)  
- Bharti Airtel plans to scale Nxtra’s data‑centre capacity to about 1 GW and then beyond, implying a multi‑year capex curve in digital infrastructure.  
- Management clarified that this investment will not involve GPUs; focus is on carrier‑grade, colocation, and cloud‑adjacent infrastructure, not deep‑AI compute stacks.  
- Separately, Airtel is backing a 1‑GW AI‑focused hyperscale hub in Andhra Pradesh with Google Cloud (total ~$15bn project over 2026–30), but this is treated as a partner‑driven project, not a standalone GPU‑centric venture.  

Financial services (Airtel Money / NBFC)  
- Airtel has earmarked ₹20,000 crore over several years for its financial‑services business, judged sufficient to scale the NBFC to self‑sustaining levels.  
- First‑year capital outlay is expected at 10–15% of the total, with roughly 70% funded by the listed entity and the balance from other Bharti sources.  
- The initial target TAM is Bharti’s top 100 million subscribers, focusing on payments, credit, and savings, with monetisation ramping gradually rather than in Year 1.  

Africa and group‑level moves  
- Bharti is open to expansion in other African markets or globally, but the intent is to take strategic operational stakes (to run businesses) rather than pure financial stakes.  
- Bharti Enterprises’ existing ~16% stake in Bharti Airtel Africa could migrate into Bharti Airtel Ltd. over time, though no concrete timeline was given.  
- An ~8% promoter stake sale in Bharti Airtel is expected over the medium‑term (3–4 years) via Bharti Telecom Ltd. (BTL) and Singtel, with BTL eventually becoming the sole promoter entity.  

Market‑structure view  
- Given 8–9% sector growth, Mittal sees limited realistic scope for a balance‑sheet‑strained player to gain meaningful share without a structural reset.  
- For Airtel, that reinforces pricing discipline, network‑quality bias, and ARPU‑up pathways, with Jio’s moves as the primary benchmark on pricing and bundles.  

Key takeaway:  
Bharti Airtel is signalling a disciplined, multi‑year capital‑allocation mix: double down on core India–Africa telecom, selectively scale Nxtra and fintech, and steadily raise dividends—making for a rare, shareholder‑oriented compounder in Indian infra–tech.

🛢️ OPEC+ Approves Gradual Output Hike of 206 kb/d ; Reports 

• 📈 Production Increase: OPEC+ core group agreed to raise output by 206,000 barrels/day — a measured, phased move.

• 🧮 Calibrated Unwind: Increase equals roughly 1.5-month step, signalling controlled supply return — not a flood.

• ⏳ Cut Reversal Timeline: With 1.032 mb/d still curtailed, full unwind of the 1.65 mb/d voluntary cut may take ~5 months at this pace.

• 👀 High Vigilance: Group shifting toward day-by-day market monitoring amid geopolitical uncertainty.

• ⚖️ Optionality Preserved: OPEC+ retaining flexibility to adjust supply quickly if market conditions change.

🟡 Impact: Neutral to Slightly Negative for Oil Prices
Gradual supply increase may cap crude upside, but controlled pace limits risk of sharp price fall.

Small Caps: Cheap for a Reason? Data Says Yes (But Look Closer)

Market Cap vs Drawdown & EV/EBITDA  
- < ₹2,000 Cr → -33% drawdown | 11.3x  
- ₹2,000–5,000 Cr → -29% | 13x  
- ₹5,000–10,000 Cr → -26% | 16.5x  
- ₹10,000–25,000 Cr → -22% | 17.7x  
- ₹25,000–50,000 Cr → -20% | 22.3x  
- > ₹50,000 Cr → -12.6% | 21.1x  

Clear Pattern  
- Smaller company → deeper fall (26–33% corrections)  
- Smaller company → lower valuation multiple (11–16x)  
- Larger company → shallower correction (~13–20%)  
- Larger company → richer valuations (~21–22x)  

Translation  
- Small caps already corrected 26–33% from 52W highs  
- Now trade at 11–16x EV/EBITDA  
- Large caps corrected ~13–20% at ~21–22x  

Key Takeaway: Small caps look cheap post-correction but deeper drawdowns signal higher risk – not a free lunch despite the valuation gap.

**Gravita India (CMP: 1,594): Strategic Expansions Fuel Multi-Year Compounder Story**

**Strategic Moves**  
- Acquired Rashtriya Metals Industries (RMIL) for Rs 565 crore – entry into copper recycling/alloys; total copper capacity now 340 ktpa, +45 ktpa commissioning this quarter, fully onstream by Q2FY27.  
- Mundra lead recycling expanded to 145,100 TPA (Rs 49 crore capex via internal accruals).  
- 6,000 TPA Li-ion battery recycling plant commissioned at Mundra (Rs 14 crore capex) – taps EV/clean energy circular economy theme.

**Q3FY26 Performance**  
- Revenue Rs 1,017 crore (+2.1% YoY); EBITDA Rs 111 crore (+25.2% YoY, OPM 11.0% up 200 bps); PAT Rs 98 crore (+25.3% YoY), EPS Rs 13.4.  
- Lead core driver (85-90% volumes/revenue): Q3 lead revenue +9% YoY to Rs 915 crore; EBITDA/kg Rs 23 (+21% YoY); guides 18-20% lead growth, EBITDA/kg Rs 19-20 stable.  
- Aluminium weak (-33% revenue YoY, -43% volumes); plastics revenue -39% YoY but EBITDA/kg improves to Rs 10.

**Growth Outlook**  
- Q4FY26 volume growth guided at 8-9%; long-term revenue/APAT CAGRs ~18%/~26% with RoCE ~18%.  
- Vision 2029: 25%+ volume CAGR, 35%+ profitability CAGR FY25-FY29; 50% revenue from value-added products by FY29.  
- Tailwinds from rising scrap availability, BMWR/EPR/RCM policies, EV/clean energy demand favoring Mundra/new lines.

**Valuation & View**  
- Trades at 33x/27x FY27E/FY28E EPS; FY28E EPS Rs 71.3, RoCE ~18%, RoNW 17-18%.  
- Mirae Asset Sharekhan: Buy, TP Rs 2,000 (28x FY28E EPS, ~25% upside).

**Key Risks**  
- Regulatory issues around scrap procurement and policy changes impacting sourcing.  
- Global aluminium price volatility tightening scrap availability/processing volumes.

**Key Takeaway**: Gravita's copper/Li-ion push atop lead strength sets up 25%+ volume CAGR to FY29, but execution amid aluminium volatility and regulatory risks will determine re-rating potential.

With today’s US–Israel strikes on Iran, focus shifts to the Strait of Hormuz. Tehran has previously threatened to close this vital energy lifeline that moves a fifth of global oil. Any disruption could halt Gulf exports, hit Asia and Europe hard, and rattle global supply chains.

US–Israel strikes on Iran put the Strait of Hormuz at the center of global risk. Just 21 miles wide, it carries ~21 mb/d of oil, 20–25% of global seaborne trade, and a third of LNG. Many nations depend on it, and any further escalation could send fuel prices soaring worldwide.

Vintage Coffee & Beverages Ltd – Management Meet Highlights

Capacity & Expansion

• Current capacity: 4,500 MT → 6,500 MT
• 2,000 MT added. Fully utilised within 1 month
• Ongoing brownfield: +4,500 MT → Total 11,000 MT by FY27
• Greenfield FDC: 5,500 MT. Launch Mar/Apr 2027
• FDC expected ~70% utilisation in Year 1
• Owns 23 acres. ~12 acres available for future expansion

Capex Plan

• 2,000 MT expansion: ₹15 Cr
• 4,500 MT brownfield: ₹45 Cr
• FDC greenfield: ₹200–220 Cr
• Funding largely through internal accruals + project funding

Realisation & Margin Profile

• Spray-dried: ~₹700/kg
• Agglomerated: ~₹730/kg
• FDC: >₹1,000/kg
• Bulk EBITDA: 15–16%
• Consumer packs: 18–19%
• FDC EBITDA: 23–24%

Clear premium in FDC economics.

Product Mix Shift

• Earlier: 70% bulk / 30% consumer
• Now: 50% bulk / 50% consumer
• Target: 60–70% consumer mix
• Consumer packs offer 2–3% higher margin
• Realisation better by 20–30%
• Currently 100% white-labelled

Margin expansion visible with mix change.

Seasonality

• H1 contributes ~40% revenue
• H2 contributes ~60% revenue
• Q3–Q4 peak season

Market & Order Visibility

• 100% export focused
• Key markets: Europe, Middle East, Russia
• LOIs for 4,000+ MT FDC
• No exposure to US or Iran

Strong FDC demand visibility.

Domestic Strategy

• Limited India presence currently
• Structured entry in 3–4 years
• India coffee consumption growing 8–10%

Industry View

• Instant coffee global growth: 2–3%
• FDC growth: 7–8%
• Prices stable near cost floor
• Not concerned about expansion by CCL Products (India) Ltd or Tata Coffee Ltd

Key Investor Takeaway

• Capacity expansion from 6,500 MT → 11,000 MT + 5,500 MT FDC
• Premium FDC margins at 23–24%
• Consumer mix shift driving structural margin uplift
• Clear order visibility before commissioning

Growth roadmap is capacity-led with improving margin mix from FY27 onwards.

South Korea has been on a tear. India continues to lag global markets.

Source: Neil Borate

**Why Businesses Fail: 2 Simple Financial Rules**

**Rule 1: ROCE > Cost of Capital**  
Money costs 12-15% (equity/debt).  
**ROCE 10% vs CoC 14% = 4% value destruction annually.**  
Sustainable wealth only when ROCE consistently exceeds CoC.  

**Rule 2: Cash In Before Cash Out**  
Profits ≠ cash.  
**Short-term debt for long-term assets creates repayment mismatches.**  
OCF must cover debt servicing needs for stability.  

**Simple Test**  
- ROCE > CoC → **Value creation**  
- OCF ≥ Debt needs → **Financial stability**  

**Key Takeaway:**  
90% failures trace to these gaps. Master both rules = survive cycles, compound wealth.
A 1 MW data center needs 68,500 liters of water a day.

• Received appellate orders from Commissioner of Income Tax (Appeals)–27, New Delhi

Next Step by Company

Investor Takeaway
• Major relief with expected reduction of tax exposure from ₹84.89 Cr to ₹6.11 Cr
• Significant reduction in contingent liability risk
• Financial overhang largely resolved, subject to final procedural orders and ITAT outcome

**Sakar Healthcare Ltd – Oncology-Led Re‑rating Story (Educational)**  

**Oncology Growth Engine**  
- Oncology revenues are projected to jump from ~₹33 Cr in FY25 to ~₹350 Cr by FY28, implying ~118% CAGR.  
- This materially shifts the mix towards higher-margin, specialty products and can drive a step-up in overall profitability.  

**Integrated Oncology Platform**  
- Vertically integrated model with in-house API and formulation in oncology supports better margins, supply security, and stronger pricing power.  
- This integration also improves control over quality and regulatory compliance – crucial for global oncology play.  

**Regulatory & Market Expansion**  
- Target of 250–300 Marketing Authorisations (MAs) by FY27 across 48 countries builds a diversified global franchise.  
- Deeper penetration in EU plus emerging markets reduces single-region risk and expands the addressable market.  

**Partnership Upside (Accord Healthcare)**  
- Supply agreement with Accord Healthcare for EU oncology portfolio carries medium-term revenue potential of >₹450 Cr.  
- Successful scale-up under this partnership can accelerate utilisation ramp-up and validate Sakar as a credible global oncology supplier.  

**Capacity Operating Leverage**  
- Bavla oncology plant has revenue potential of up to ~₹1,000 Cr at ~80% utilisation with no major additional capex.  
- As volumes ramp, fixed-cost absorption should boost margins, RoCE and operating cash flows.  

**Base Business Cushion**  
- Non-oncology CMO/CDMO business provides a stable base, smoothing earnings while oncology scales up.  
- This offers downside support if ramp-up in oncology is slower than expected.  

**Valuation Context**  
- At ~15x FY28E earnings, the stock is baking in strong growth but still reasonable versus the scale of oncology option value, provided execution/regulatory milestones are met.  

**Key Takeaway:**  
Sakar Healthcare is pivoting from a stable CMO/CDMO base to a high-growth, integrated global oncology player, with capacity already in place and multiple triggers (MAs, Accord partnership, utilisation) that can drive earnings and potential re-rating over FY25–28, subject to execution and regulatory delivery.

**Small and MIDs Performance In RBI Rate Cut Cycles**

**During Rate Cuts**
• Returns were mixed
• Midcap & Smallcap delivered negative returns in some cycles
• Volatility remained high

**After Rate Cut Cycle Ends**
• SMIDs outperformed meaningfully
• Strong recovery phase observed
• Liquidity and growth improved

**Historical Trend (25 Years)**
• Every rate cut cycle above **100 bps** followed by sharp rebound
• Consistent recovery across macro environments
• Strong **2-year absolute returns** post easing

**Why It Happens**
• Lower rates boost liquidity
• Improves earnings growth visibility
• Drives valuation re-rating
• Works better when starting valuations are reasonable

**Investor Insight**
• Bigger opportunity often **after easing phase**
• Patience during cuts, positioning for recovery
• SMIDs benefit most in liquidity-driven upcycles

Banks May Raise CD & Bulk Deposit Rates Amid Liquidity Squeeze

Funding Cost Pressure
• Banks likely to raise CD and bulk deposit rates in March
• Driven by year-end credit demand
• Liquidity conditions tightening

CD Rate Trend
• 3-month CD rates crossed 7% (~7.10%)
• May rise toward 7.50% if loan demand sustains
• Short-term borrowing becoming costlier

Credit-Deposit Stress
• Credit-deposit ratio at ~82% (record high)
• Reflects elevated funding pressure
• Loan growth outpacing deposit mobilisation

Liquidity Conditions
• System liquidity around ₹2.50 lakh crore in February
• March-end balance sheet adjustments add strain
• GST outflows impacting liquidity

Regulatory & Structural Factors
• Lower Liquidity Coverage Ratio (LCR) buffer
• Elevated loan-to-deposit ratios
• Greater dependence on short-term instruments

Market Influence
• Debt mutual fund flows critical for liquidity
• January inflows improved after December outflows
• Volatility in flows can impact rates

Investor Perspective
• Funding cost pressure may compress NIMs near term
• Deposit competition likely to intensify
• CASA mobilisation becomes critical
• Temporary spike possible till March-end

Short-term liquidity tightness may persist, keeping funding costs elevated into fiscal year-end.

Advanced Packaging Seen As India’s Entry Into Global Chip Supply Chains

Strategic Opportunity
• Advanced packaging identified as key integration point
• Moves India beyond basic assembly and testing
• Aligns with global semiconductor value chain shifts

Technology Transition
• Shift from pure lithography scaling
• Rising focus on materials engineering
• Growth in 3D chips and heterogeneous integration
• Advanced packaging gaining strategic importance

India Advantage
• Strong semiconductor design talent pool
• Large engineering workforce
• Faster execution critical to capture opportunity

Applied Materials Expansion
• Scaling 300 mm wafer India Validation Center
• New facility inaugurated in Bengaluru
• Committed $400 million over four years for collaborative engineering centre

Global Demand Context
• AI-led semiconductor demand remains strong
• Over 100 new fabs being tracked globally
• Structural multi-year growth cycle intact

Challenges
• Power availability constraints
• Infrastructure readiness gaps
• Need for ecosystem-level coordination

Investor Perspective
• Advanced packaging offers lower capex entry vs full-scale fabs
• Opportunity to build niche, high-value positioning
• Beneficiary segments: materials, equipment, testing solutions
• Long-term strategic play in global semiconductor supply chain

Advanced packaging can serve as India’s pragmatic and scalable gateway into next-generation semiconductor manufacturing.

RE Ministry Plans Ancillary Market Push For Grid Stability

Policy Initiative
• MNRE to initiate discussions on ancillary market framework
• Issue taken up with Central Electricity Authority
• Aim to address frequency and voltage fluctuations

Key Focus Areas
• Frequency control services
• Voltage support mechanisms
• Black start capability
• Grid-forming inverters deployment
• Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) integration

Trigger For Action
• Rapid renewable capacity additions
• Rising grid integration challenges
• Need for structured system reserves

Market Development
• CEA preparing paper on ancillary service opportunities
• Potential new revenue stream for service providers
• Formal market-based compensation model likely

Infrastructure Alignment
• Transmission expansion being prioritised
• Grid strengthening alongside renewable rollout
• Focus on long-term system reliability

Structural Context
• India now 3rd largest solar market globally
• Renewables forming rising share of installed capacity
• Grid modernisation becoming critical

Investor Perspective
• Positive for BESS and grid technology players
• Opens ancillary revenue pool for power companies
• Improves long-term renewable integration stability
• Supports sustainable capacity expansion

Structured ancillary market development is key to balancing India’s accelerating renewable growth trajectory.

US Solar Duty Pushes SEZ Units Toward Domestic Market

US Trade Action
• US imposed 125.87% countervailing duty on Indian solar imports
• Preliminary decision; final ruling by July 6, 2026
• May change if bilateral trade deal materialises

Export Exposure
• India exported $1.2 billion solar panels to US in 2025
• Down from $1.5 billion in 2024
• US accounts for 98% of India’s solar exports
• High concentration risk highlighted

Immediate Impact
• US importers must post duty deposits
• Landed cost increases significantly
• Risk of margin pressure and order cancellations
• Contract disputes possible for committed shipments

Industry Response
• Industry bodies seek permission for SEZ units to sell domestically
• Aim to offset export disruption
• Push to diversify revenue streams

Strategic Implication
• Reduces over-dependence on single geography
• Strengthens case for domestic solar demand absorption
• Aligns with India’s renewable capacity expansion plans

Legal Angle
• WTO challenge possible
• Litigation process likely prolonged
• Near-term uncertainty persists

Investor Perspective
• Near-term earnings risk for export-focused players
• Domestic market shift could cushion revenue impact
• Policy support critical for SEZ flexibility
• Sector volatility expected until final US determination

Trade action increases concentration risk but may accelerate domestic market integration for Indian solar manufacturers.

reports.text:
KFin Technologies: IFSCA Grants TechFin Registration to Subsidiary

• 🏦 Regulatory Approval: IFSCA granted Certificate of Registration to KFin Global Technologies (IFSC), a wholly owned subsidiary. 

• ⚙️ New Business Capability: Subsidiary can now operate as a TechFin and Ancillary Services Provider under IFSCA regulations. 

• 🌍 IFSC Opportunity: Strengthens presence in GIFT City ecosystem and expands fintech service offerings.

• 📊 Financial Impact: Company stated no immediate financial implications from the approval. 

🟢 Impact: Positive
Regulatory clearance enhances KFintech’s fintech positioning in IFSC/GIFT City and opens new growth avenues, though near-term financial impact is limited.

Lupin: Goa Facility Gets USFDA EIR with VAI Classification

• 🧪 Regulatory Milestone: Lupin received Establishment Inspection Report (EIR) from USFDA for its Goa facility. 

• ✅ VAI Status: Inspection concluded with Voluntary Action Indicated (VAI), indicating satisfactory compliance. 

• 🌍 US Market Comfort: Outcome removes major regulatory overhang for the Goa plant.

• 📈 Quality Focus Reinforced: Management highlighted continued commitment to quality and compliance. 

🟢 Impact: Positive
Favourable USFDA outcome de-risks the Goa facility, supports US business continuity, and improves regulatory sentiment for Lupin.

GPT Infraprojects: Acquires Alcon Builders to Enter Rail Signalling EPC

• 🏗️ Strategic Acquisition: Company acquired 100% stake in Alcon Builders & Engineers for ₹151.83 crore. 

• 🚆 New Segment Entry: Move marks entry into high-margin rail signalling EPC business, complementing existing railway exposure. 

• 📈 Synergy Potential: Management expects integration to strengthen EPC portfolio and drive long-term growth. 

• 🎯 Market Opportunity: Positions GPT to capture future railway capex opportunities in a niche segment. 

🟢 Impact: Positive
Strategic diversification into rail signalling EPC with full ownership is growth-accretive and improves long-term revenue visibility.

DGCA Introduces 48-Hour Free Cancellation Window

New Passenger-Friendly Rule
• 48-hour free cancellation and modification window introduced
• Applicable if flight is 7 days away (domestic)
• Applicable if flight is 15 days away (international)
• Effective from March 26

Refund Framework
• Airlines must process refunds within 14 days
• Credit card refunds within 7 working days
• Applies to bookings via airlines or travel agents

Cancellation Charge Cap
• Fees cannot exceed basic fare + fuel surcharge
• No excessive penalty charges allowed
• Clear disclosure of refund break-up mandatory

Credit Shell Option
• Passengers may opt for credit shell vouchers
• Provides flexibility instead of cash refund
• Transferable in medical emergency cases

Medical Emergency Clause
• Refund or transferable credit shell permitted
• Enhances passenger protection norms

Regulatory Objective
• Improve transparency in airline pricing
• Reduce passenger complaints
• Standardise refund timelines across carriers

Investor Perspective
• May impact ancillary fee income for airlines
• Improves customer trust and brand perception
• Short-term revenue adjustment, long-term structural benefit
• Regulatory tightening focused on consumer protection

Move strengthens passenger rights while formalising refund discipline across the aviation sector.

Royal Orchid Targets Asset-Light Growth With Strategic Tie-Ups

Strategic Partnership Approach
• Open to global tie-ups on Marriott / Accor-like model
• Focus only if shareholder value accretive
• Partnership-led brand premium possible

Expansion Target
• Vision to reach 250 hotels and 20,000 keys by 2030
• Current portfolio at ~168 hotels and 10,700 keys
• Strong multi-year scale-up roadmap

Asset-Light Strategy
• Expansion via management contracts
• Brownfield conversions preferred
• Limited capital-intensive greenfield projects
• Higher return on capital focus

Brand Portfolio Optimization
• Five clearly defined brand segments
• Regenta positioned as primary growth engine
• Brand sharpening to improve pricing power

Pipeline Visibility
• Around 47 hotels under development
• Majority through asset conversions
• Faster execution and improved capital efficiency

Geographic Focus
• India remains core growth market
• Limited priority to international expansion
• Convention-driven demand seen rising in Delhi

Capital Allocation Discipline
• No aggressive debt-led expansion
• No large PE-driven capital raises
• Selective and calibrated investments

Investor Perspective
• Asset-light model improves ROCE profile
• Controlled leverage reduces balance sheet risk
• Strong domestic demand tailwinds
• Scalable platform with margin expansion potential

Company positioning itself for steady, capital-efficient growth with long-term visibility.

Govt Mandates Nationwide Sale Of E20 Petrol From April

Regulatory Directive
• Sale of petrol blended with up to 20% ethanol (E20) mandated from April 1
• Minimum RON 95 specification required
• Applicable across all states and Union Territories
• Directive issued by Ministry of Petroleum and Natural Gas

Technical Aspect
• RON measures resistance to engine knocking
• Higher RON ensures better performance and efficiency
• Most new vehicles compatible with E20 blending
• Blending beyond 20% requires engineering changes

Strategic Objective
• Reduce crude oil import dependence
• Strengthen India’s energy security
• Boost domestic ethanol production capacity

Industry Impact
• Provides long-term demand visibility for ethanol producers
• Positive for sugar mills and grain-based distilleries
• Supports farmers growing sugarcane and maize
• Enhances rural income ecosystem

Economic Implication
• Ethanol blending helping reduce oil import bill
• Structural shift toward biofuel integration
• Strengthens domestic energy value chain

Investor Perspective
• Positive for ethanol manufacturers and integrated sugar players
• Improves revenue stability through policy-backed demand
• Long-term structural tailwind for agri-linked energy businesses

Policy move reinforces India’s biofuel roadmap with nationwide implementation clarity.

Govt Plans Value-Addition Linked PLI For Smartphones

Policy Shift In PLI Structure
• Next phase of smartphone PLI may link incentives to domestic value addition
• Current scheme based on incremental sales ends March 31
• Proposed model ties payouts to predefined localisation thresholds

Current Scenario
• Exports, value addition, employment monitored but not mandatory
• Domestic value addition improved to 35–40% by FY26-end
• Initially started at 15–20%
• Still below earlier 40% expectation

Strategic Objective
• Move beyond basic assembly operations
• Push deeper localisation of components
• Encourage manufacturing of production equipment locally

Ecosystem Development
• Separate electronics components manufacturing scheme launched
• Focus on strengthening domestic supply chain
• Reduce import dependency over time

Structural Transition
• Shift from volume-driven incentives
• Move toward “beyond assembly” strategy
• Aim to build complete electronics ecosystem in India

Investor Takeaway
• Favors players with strong localisation capabilities
• Likely to benefit component manufacturers
• Margin profile may improve with deeper value chain integration
• Long-term positive for India’s electronics manufacturing story

Policy direction clearly signals quality and depth over pure output growth.

reports.text:
Apple In Talks With Indian Banks For Apple Pay Launch

Proposed Rollout Timeline
• Discussions underway with ICICI Bank, HDFC Bank, and Axis Bank
• Expected launch around Mid-2026
• Early-stage ecosystem integration

Payment Infrastructure Strategy
• Likely support for UPI and card-based payments
• Alignment with India’s digital payments backbone
• Engagement with Mastercard and Visa

Market Opportunity
• India has 750+ million smartphone users
• Among fastest-growing digital payments markets
• Strong structural shift toward cashless economy

Competitive Landscape
• Dominated by Google Pay, PhonePe, Amazon Pay, and Paytm
• Apple likely to focus on premium segment first
• Ecosystem-driven adoption strategy

Technology Differentiation
• Face ID / Touch ID based authentication
• Tap-to-pay and seamless online transactions
• High security and privacy positioning

Strategic India Focus
• Part of broader India expansion strategy
• Expanding local manufacturing and retail footprint
• India emerging as key iPhone production hub
• Supply chain diversification beyond China

Investor Perspective
• Strengthens Apple services revenue play
• Enhances ecosystem stickiness in India
• Long-term structural growth opportunity
• Competitive but high-growth market

India remains central to Apple’s next phase of global expansion.

@Wealthcreator7

NSE Chief Pushes F&O Eligibility Norms

Proposal Overview  
- NSE MD Ashish Chauhan calls for minimum qualifying criteria for F&O trading  
- Aims to protect lower-income investors from speculative losses  

Global Benchmark  
- US model: $25,000 minimum margin balance required  
- India currently: No eligibility threshold  

Rationale  
- Curb excessive retail speculation  
- Align with Singapore's stricter access norms  

Regulatory Context  
- Follows Budget STT hikes: Equity futures to 0.05% (from 0.02%), options premium to 0.15% (from 0.1%)  
- Exchanges/regulators tightening derivatives access  

Strategic Intent  
- Enhanced risk management focus  
- Promotes derivatives market stability  

Key Takeaway:  
NSE's eligibility push signals multi-pronged retail protection strategy — F&O participation set for quality-over-quantity shift.

@Stockinfo333

Mahindra's New Born-Electric SUV: 2027 Launch

Model Details  
- Internally codenamed B07  
- Based on BE.07 concept (showcased 2022)  
- New marketing name at launch  

Production Plans  
- Built at Chakan plant (Maharashtra)  
- Plant undergoing capacity expansion for EV models  

Strategic Role  
- Part of 2027 EV scale-up strategy  
- Expected key volume driver  

Current EV Outlook  
- Existing 3 models: 7,000–8,000 monthly sales  
- Annual run-rate: ~80,000 units  

Key Takeaway:  
Mahindra bolsters born-electric portfolio with B07 — capacity expansion positions it for sustained EV volume leadership.

Ather Under Scale Pressure as TVS & Bajaj Accelerate EV Push

Market Snapshot  
- CY2025 EV retail: 2.27 mn units; e-2Ws = 56% share  
- Ather hits 500k cumulative sales; Q3FY26 volumes +50% YoY to 68k units (18.8% market share)  

Leadership Battle  
- TVS iQube: 29% share, ~40k monthly output, 300k CY2025 sales  
- Bajaj Chetak 2501: ₹87,100 aggressive pricing, 11.9k early-Feb sales (23% share)  
- Hero Vida: ₹73,850-₹1.4L, 5.7k early-Feb units  

Ather's Regional Edge & Gaps  
- South dominance: 24.4% share  
- Lags rivals' 400+ stores, 4k touchpoints across 800 cities  
- Rizta model: Drives 70% volumes via battery-as-a-service  

Incumbents' Advantages  
- ICE cash flows fund EV scale  
- Superior procurement leverage  
- Extensive dealer networks  

Ather's Counter-Moves  
- Hosuru plant: 420k annual capacity  
- New Maharashtra facility lifts total to 1.42 mn at full ramp  
- EL platform mass entry (₹1-1.25L) targets North India later 2026  

Key Takeaway:  
Scale economics favor TVS/Bajaj's network depth; Ather's capacity ramp and mass-market pivot critical to defend premium positioning.

@Stocksip

Railways Real Estate Monetisation: ₹1.4 Lakh Cr Target in 5 Years

NMP 2.0 Breakdown  
- Total target: ₹1.4 lakh crore (FY26–FY30)  
- ₹1 lakh crore from station redevelopment  
- ₹43,800 crore from other railway land via PPP  

Station Redevelopment Plan  
- 200 stations transformed into "City Centres" with commercial, retail, amenities  
- OMT model: Operate-Maintain-Transfer with revenue sharing  
- Ministry of Railways oversight  

Annual Targets  
- Stations: FY26: ₹21,591 cr, FY27: ₹22,045 cr, FY28: ₹9,659 cr, FY29: ₹30,114 cr, FY30: ₹16,591 cr  
- Other land: FY26: ₹7,000 cr, FY27: ₹11,400 cr, FY28: ₹10,700 cr, FY29: ₹5,500 cr, FY30: ₹9,200 cr  

Early Progress  
- FY26 YTD: ₹9,890 crore already achieved by RLDA  
- Real estate = 54% of Railways' ₹2.6 lakh cr NMP 2.0 goal  

Strategic Focus  
- Transit-oriented development  
- Multimodal connectivity enhancement  
- Urban surplus land commercialisation  

Key Takeaway:  
Railways' aggressive station monetisation fuels infra capex recycling — major opportunity for real estate and PPP execution plays.

GAIL's repeated cuts signal margin pressures persisting — capex adds execution risks.

States Cut Debt But 20% FRBM Target Elusive

Debt Trajectory Improving  
- FY25 (RE): 28.4% debt-to-GSDP (down from 31.0% FY21 peak)  
- Historical: FY15: 21.7%, FY20: 26.6%, FY24: 28.1%, FY26 (BE): 29.2%  

Interest Burden Eases  
- Interest payments as revenue receipts share moderated post-Covid despite high debt levels  

High-Debt States (FY25 RE)  
- Punjab: 46.9%  
- West Bengal: 40%  
- Bihar: 38%  
- Rajasthan: 37%  
- Kerala: 36.2%  
- Andhra Pradesh: 35.5%  
- Madhya Pradesh: 30.8%  

Prudent States  
- Gujarat: 17.8%  
- Maharashtra: 18.6%  
- Odisha: 20.6%  
- Telangana: 27.3%  

Key Takeaway:  
Debt reduction underway via GDP growth, but structural consolidation needed for 20% FRBM target — watch high-debt states' fiscal discipline.

India C&I Renewable Energy Capacity To Reach 57 GW By FY28

Capacity Expansion Outlook

• C&I renewable capacity projected at 57 GW by FY28
• Expected to reach ~40 GW by FY26
• Implies strong multi-year growth visibility

Growth Drivers

• Corporate decarbonisation and net-zero commitments
• Tariff arbitrage between open-access RE and grid tariffs
• Long-term PPAs improving revenue visibility
• Renewable Purchase Obligations (RPOs) compliance push
• Attractive IRR for developers

Policy & Regulatory Support

• Green Energy Open Access (GEOA) Rules, 2022 enabling direct sourcing
• Use of existing transmission infrastructure
• Industrial states offering fast-track approvals and incentives
• Policy clarity reducing execution risk

Demand Strength

• C&I remains India’s largest power-consuming segment
• Rising ESG focus from corporates
• Stable and strong counterparty credit profiles

Investor Takeaway

• Visible pipeline till FY28
• Long-term contracted cash flows via PPAs
• Strong policy tailwinds
• Structural shift toward clean energy adoption

Medium-term opportunity remains robust for developers focused on C&I renewable portfolio expansion.

SEBI Revamps MF Rules: Gold/Silver Exposure Upto 35%

Expanded Asset Flexibility  
- Equity funds can allocate upto 35% to gold, silver, InvITs  
- Moves beyond traditional equity/debt instruments  

Solution Schemes Frozen  
- No fresh inflows into retirement/children's funds (₹60,000 cr AUM)  
- Schemes to merge with similar allocation profiles  

Stricter Categorisation  
- Multi-cap funds: Minimum 25% each in large/mid/small-cap  
- Sectoral/thematic overlap capped at 50% with other equity schemes  
- Overlap calculated by common securities + weightage  

Investor Protection Focus  
- Ensures "true to label" scheme positioning  
- Curbs mis-selling and scheme duplication  

Strategic Timing  
- Boosts commodity diversification amid global gold/silver rally  

Key Takeaway:  
SEBI's reforms enhance flexibility while enforcing discipline — gold/silver access positions equity funds for uncertainty.

L&T Finance Addresses Bihar MFI Bill Concerns

Portfolio Exposure  
- Bihar represents reasonable part of overall book  
- 17% of MFI portfolio exposed to state  

Expected Impact  
- High collection efficiency minimises material hit  
- Still early days on MFI bill effects  

Regulatory Outlook  
- Bill likely fosters more responsible lending  

AI Strategy Pillars  
- Focus: Efficiency, experience, education  
- ₹110 cr invested (Cyclops ₹80 cr, Nostradamus ₹30 cr)  
- Target: Slash underwriting from 40 to 10 hours in 6 months  
- Needs robust digital infrastructure buildout  

Key Takeaway:  
Collections buffer regulatory risks while AI investments drive underwriting efficiency and scalable growth.

**Citi on India Brokers & Asset Managers: SEBI MF Reforms Impact**

SEBI's **MF categorisation overhaul** drives scheme rationalisation.

**Key Changes:**  
- **(1) Discontinue solution-oriented schemes** (₹0.6 tn AUM, 0.7% of Jan'26 total) — merge into similar schemes post approval  
- **(2) Introduce 'Life Cycle Funds'** — open-ended with maturity, glide path across equity/debt/InvITs/ETCDs/gold-silver ETF  
- **(3) Allow value & contra schemes** if overlap <50% (currently limited to SBI/Kotak/Invesco)  
- **(4) Cap sectoral/thematic overlap** at 50% with other equity (ex-large cap); comply within **3 years**

**Outlook:**  
Expect **fewer new sectoral schemes**.

**Key Takeaway:**  
Reforms promote differentiation and merger activity, curbing proliferation while adding goal-based Life Cycle option.

Gem Aromatics Ltd – Plant Visit Note
(Nirmal Bang Retail Research)

*# FY28E Guidance:  Rs. 1,100 cr of Revenue with margin in the range of 16% to 18%*
*#Capacity expansion to drive the growth: recent commissioning of the Dahej MPP plant (~10k MTPA) will drive the growth over the next three to four years. Prior to Dahej, capacity stands at ~5.3k MTPA.*

*Outlook: Positive over mid-term*

*Key Highlights*
• We visited a Multi-purpose Plant (MPP) plant of Gem Aromatics Ltd located at Dahej, Gujarat on 24th Feb’26, which commissioned its phase I production of clove and clove derivatives in Nov’25, followed by Phase II production of Cooling Agents, Phenol Derivatives and Citral Derivatives in Dec’25.
• Incorporated in 1997, Gem Aromatics Ltd is one of the players in specialty chemicals industry mainly focusing on flavour & fragrance (F&F) ingredients and aroma chemicals. 
• It is an established manufacturer of specialty ingredients, including, essential oils, aroma chemicals and value-added derivatives in India. Its portfolio of 70+ products spans across mint derivatives (incl. menthol), spice & citrus derivatives, and a growing set of aroma chemicals, supplied to oral care, pharma, cosmetics, nutraceuticals, wellness and pain management and personal care across India and more than 20 countries.
• The Company is managed by Mr. Yash Parekh - MD & CEO, Mrs. Kaksha Parekh - Chairperson and Chief Financial Officer and, Mr. Vipul Parekh – Whole Time Director. Mr. Vipul looks after capacity expansion plans, while Mr. Yash deals in sales and purchases.
• Domestic: Export mix stands at 50:50; where US has a major share in exports. It is witnessing demand recovery in exports.
• It has three manufacturing facilities, located at Silvassa (1500 MTPA), Budaun (3800 MTPA) and Dahej (10829 MTPA). Total capacity stands at ~16000 MTPA. 
• It’s newly commissioned plant at Dahej is a ~80% automated and implemented vapor-phase technology for the manufacturing of Anisole with a continuous flow chemistry. In continuous flow chemistry, shift towards production of new products will require 7 days.
• In the Citral Derivatives, it has built largest capacity in the world for Safranal to compete with global major like Givaudan for import substitution.
• In the plant area – it has an admin building, an area with 21 tanks processing various solvents as per requirement, a warehouse which can store ~3500 drums underground, a process plant area of 25K sq. ft. which has a 5-level building and boiler rooms. In the Warehouse, storage capacity stands at ~4-4.5K KGS, which includes raw material, work in progress, semi-finished goods and finished goods.
• It highlighted that Government has made a provision Common Effluent Treatment Plant (CETP) at Dahej location, which offers savings from developing a Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) plant at a cost of Rs. 15 per 1000 litre of water that goes into CETP. Also, safety is at the core with all necessary provisions in place.
• Clove and Clove Derivatives production is based on the batch process, which requires at least 2 days to shift the production of a new product.
• Product category wise FY28E revenue guidance for Dahej plant: Citral and Citral Derivatives - Rs. 50 cr, Phenol and Phenol Derivatives – Rs. 250 cr, Clove and Clove Derivatives – Rs. 150 cr and Cooling Agents – Rs. 175 cr and other specialty chemicals + CDMO + installed MPP – Rs. 175 cr.
• It has sourced all the required equipment locally and developed this MPP project at Dahej.
• So far, it has completed 8 technology transfers, which are at the initial stage and generated ~Rs. 3-3.5 cr of revenue from these green products. It is focusing on products which has a potential market size of the 50-1000 MTPA range.
• It has indicated that ~38 products are being ready and will be starting production in Q1FY27.

SEBI introduces Life Cycle Funds. 

A new Life Cycle Fund category replaces existing Solution Oriented Funds (Retirement & Children’s Funds).

What are Life Cycle Funds?

Life Cycle Funds will:
•Have a defined target maturity ranging from 30 years to 5 years
•Follow a pre-determined glide path asset allocation 
•Automatically reduce equity exposure as maturity date approaches
•Increase allocation to debt and safer instruments over time
•Operate as open-ended schemes with a specified maturity

Target Maturities allowed (As per SEBI framework)

Life Cycle Funds can be structured across different target maturities such as:
•30 years
•25 years
•20 years
•15 years
•10 years
•5 years

This allows investors to choose a fund aligned to their retirement horizon.

What’s in it for investors:
•Removes static allocation problem of old retirement funds 
•Aligns risk to life stage
•Reduces emotional asset allocation decisions
•removes taxation issue in existing solution oriented funds when investors switch funds to change asset allocation

20 Key Changes in SEBI’s New MF Scheme Categorisation Circular

1.AMCs can now launch both Value and Contra Funds, with portfolio overlap capped at 50%. Earlier, only one was allowed.

2.New Sectoral Debt Funds introduced (Financial Services, Energy, Infrastructure, Housing, Real Estate) min 80% in AA+ & above.

3.New Life Cycle Funds category, six maturities (30, 25, 20, 15, 10, 5 years) with fixed glide path reducing equity over time.

4.Sectoral/Thematic funds can be launched only from the list notified by AMFI in consultation with SEBI (updated half-yearly)

5.Balanced Hybrid Funds allowed with 40-60% equity allocation. Earlier, AMCs had to choose between Balanced or Aggressive Hybrid.

6.Solution-Oriented Schemes (Retirement & Children’s Plans) discontinued, fresh subscriptions stopped and schemes to be merged with similar categories.

7.Mandatory monthly disclosure of category-wise portfolio overlap (equity vs equity, debt vs debt, hybrid vs hybrid) on AMC websites.

8.Equity schemes may deploy residual allocation in money market/liquid instruments, gold/silver instruments, and InvITs.

9.Sectoral/Thematic schemes must limit portfolio overlap to 50% with other equity schemes (except large-cap). Calculated quarterly as average of daily overlap.

10.Existing Sectoral/Thematic schemes get 3 years to comply; non-compliant schemes must merge.

11.Arbitrage Funds’ debt exposure restricted to government securities (<1-year maturity) and repo in government bonds only.

12.Low Duration Fund renamed to Ultra Short to Short Term Fund.

13.Long Duration Fund renamed to Long Term Fund.

14.Dynamic Bond Fund renamed to Dynamic Term Fund.

15.Balanced Advantage Fund renamed to Dynamic Asset Allocation Fund.

16.Credit Risk Funds must invest minimum 65% in AA and below-rated bonds (earlier: below AA+).

17.Floating Rate Funds may invest in fixed-rate instruments synthetically converted to floating exposure via swaps/derivatives.

18.Hybrid schemes may invest residual allocation in InvITs (except Arbitrage), ETCDs, Gold ETFs, and Silver ETFs.

19.Equity Savings Funds must maintain net equity exposure between 15-40%.

20.Foreign securities will not be treated as a separate asset class.
